Trade Marks Act 1995 — Overview

1. Examination of Trade Mark Applications

The Trade Marks Office must examine each application for registration of a trade mark within 12 months of the application being filed. The examiner will assess whether the mark satisfies the requirements of the Act.

2. Opposition Period

Once a trade mark application is accepted, it is advertised in the Official Journal of Trade Marks. Any person may oppose the registration within 2 months of the advertisement date. An opposition must be filed in the approved form and must be accompanied by the prescribed fee of $250.

3. Registration

Upon successful completion of examination and the opposition period, the trade mark is registered for a period of 10 years. The owner must renew the registration every 10 years by paying the renewal fee.

4. Commencement

These amendments commence on 1 January 2027. The Trade Marks Amendment Act 2026 received Royal Assent on 15 November 2026.

5. Well-Known Trade Marks

A trade mark may be refused registration under section 44 of the Act if it is substantially identical or deceptively similar to a trade mark that is well known in Australia.

6. Infringement

A person infringes a registered trade mark if, without the authorisation of the registered owner, the person uses a sign as a trade mark that is substantially identical or deceptively similar to the registered trade mark in relation to goods or services in respect of which the trade mark is registered.

7. Remedies

The court may grant an injunction, award damages or an account of profits, and make orders for the delivery up or destruction of infringing goods.
